tossups.question: This author parodied the “Rousseauian Soul” in a novel whose protagonist shoots himself in the left foot to acquire the same limp as his romantic rival. In the same novel, this author depicted a solitary hunter who murders his dog Aesop and sends its corpse to Edvarda. In a different novel by this author, the protagonist gives his name as “Andreas Tangen” to a guard, and at the novel’s end leaves on a ship bound for (*) England. In that novel by this author, the protagonist is turned down for a job because he accidentally dates his application with the year 1848. In a novel by this author of Pan, a penniless writer gives a woman the name “Ylajali” (“ee-la-YAH-lee”) while wandering the streets of Christiania. For 10 points, a controversial 1945 obituary for Hitler was written by what Norwegian author of Hunger?
tossups.question_sanitized: This author parodied the "Rousseauian Soul" in a novel whose protagonist shoots himself in the left foot to acquire the same limp as his romantic rival. In the same novel, this author depicted a solitary hunter who murders his dog Aesop and sends its corpse to Edvarda. In a different novel by this author, the protagonist gives his name as "Andreas Tangen" to a guard, and at the novel's end leaves on a ship bound for (*) England. In that novel by this author, the protagonist is turned down for a job because he accidentally dates his application with the year 1848. In a novel by this author of Pan, a penniless writer gives a woman the name "Ylajali" ("ee-la-YAH-lee") while wandering the streets of Christiania. For 10 points, a controversial 1945 obituary for Hitler was written by what Norwegian author of Hunger?

tossups.question: In an article criticising Martin Heidegger, Meyer Schapiro compared this author’s descriptions of shoes with depictions by Vincent van Gogh. The protagonist of a novel by this author says that “the consciousness of being honest went to my head” after contemplating selling a blanket. That protagonist created by this author repeatedly writes “1848” on a piece of paper before trying to sell a “character sketch of Correggio.” This author controversially used the phrase “a warrior for mankind” in a 1945 (*) obituary. The protagonist tells a stranger he calls Ylajali (“ee-la-YAH-lee”) that she is losing her book in a novel by this Hitler apologist. The protagonist of a psychological novel by this author wanders around Kristiania while repeatedly both craving and rejecting food. For 10 points, name this Norwegian author of Hunger .
tossups.question_sanitized: In an article criticising Martin Heidegger, Meyer Schapiro compared this author's descriptions of shoes with depictions by Vincent van Gogh. The protagonist of a novel by this author says that "the consciousness of being honest went to my head" after contemplating selling a blanket. That protagonist created by this author repeatedly writes "1848" on a piece of paper before trying to sell a "character sketch of Correggio." This author controversially used the phrase "a warrior for mankind" in a 1945 (*) obituary. The protagonist tells a stranger he calls Ylajali ("ee-la-YAH-lee") that she is losing her book in a novel by this Hitler apologist. The protagonist of a psychological novel by this author wanders around Kristiania while repeatedly both craving and rejecting food. For 10 points, name this Norwegian author of Hunger .

tossups.question: The protagonist of a novel by this author is a wanderer who carries a violin case without a violin and a vial of prussic acid around a rural town. A farmer's wife created by this author goes to prison after she murders her third child for being born with the same harelip as her mother. The protagonist considers eating his pencil and his finger in a novel by this author, who wrote about the peasant Isak's family in The Growth of the Soil. One of this author's protagonists gives the name Ylajali (ee-la-YAH-lee) to an unknown woman and refuses a free breakfast for the homeless at the end of a novel. For 10 points, name this Norwegian author who wrote an obituary for Hitler and created a man who wanders the streets of Kristiania in their novel Hunger.
tossups.question_sanitized: The protagonist of a novel by this author is a wanderer who carries a violin case without a violin and a vial of prussic acid around a rural town. A farmer's wife created by this author goes to prison after she murders her third child for being born with the same harelip as her mother. The protagonist considers eating his pencil and his finger in a novel by this author, who wrote about the peasant Isak's family in The Growth of the Soil. One of this author's protagonists gives the name Ylajali (ee-la-YAH-lee) to an unknown woman and refuses a free breakfast for the homeless at the end of a novel. For 10 points, name this Norwegian author who wrote an obituary for Hitler and created a man who wanders the streets of Kristiania in their novel Hunger.

tossups.question: The protagonist of a novel by this author invents a fairy tale in which he visits an octagonal tower in the woods, where he sleeps in a vault filled with a million blind angels. This author’s essay “From the Unconscious Life of the Mind” called for novels to attend to “the whisper of the blood, the pleading of the bone.” In a novel by this author, the protagonist drowns trying to retrieve the mysterious iron ring he threw into the sea, shortly after berating his doppelganger, the Midget, for being secretly evil. Another novel by this author is a pastoral quasi-romance about a hunter who lives alone with his dog Aesop, Thomas Glahn. In a novel by this author of Mysteries, an unnamed young journalist gives the name “Ylajali” (“ee-la-YA-lee”) to a woman he meets while wandering the streets of Kristiania suffering from the title malady. For 10 points, name this pro-Axis Norwegian author of Pan and Hunger.
tossups.question_sanitized: The protagonist of a novel by this author invents a fairy tale in which he visits an octagonal tower in the woods, where he sleeps in a vault filled with a million blind angels. This author's essay "From the Unconscious Life of the Mind" called for novels to attend to "the whisper of the blood, the pleading of the bone." In a novel by this author, the protagonist drowns trying to retrieve the mysterious iron ring he threw into the sea, shortly after berating his doppelganger, the Midget, for being secretly evil. Another novel by this author is a pastoral quasi-romance about a hunter who lives alone with his dog Aesop, Thomas Glahn. In a novel by this author of Mysteries, an unnamed young journalist gives the name "Ylajali" ("ee-la-YA-lee") to a woman he meets while wandering the streets of Kristiania suffering from the title malady. For 10 points, name this pro-Axis Norwegian author of Pan and Hunger.

tossups.question: This author wrote about the friendship and enmity between Falkenberg and Grindhausen in two novels, Under the Autumn Stars and A Wanderer Plays on Muted Strings. He also described his imprisonment for being a Nazi sympathizer in On Overgrown Paths. His most famous novel is about a protagonist who earns money by selling a newspaper article and more from a pawnshop mistake. For 10 points, name this Nobel laureate who wrote Hunger and hailed from Norway.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author wrote about the friendship and enmity between Falkenberg and Grindhausen in two novels, Under the Autumn Stars and A Wanderer Plays on Muted Strings. He also described his imprisonment for being a Nazi sympathizer in On Overgrown Paths. His most famous novel is about a protagonist who earns money by selling a newspaper article and more from a pawnshop mistake. For 10 points, name this Nobel laureate who wrote Hunger and hailed from Norway.

tossups.question: In one novel by this author, the protagonist forgoes the vial of prussic acid that he carries in his vest pocket by leaping off a jetty. The main character befriends the cripple Miniman and chases the lieutenant’s bride Dagny in that novel by this author, who also created the entrepreneur Geissler, who staves off a drought by building an irrigation system. Besides writing a book about Johan Nagel, this author penned a novel in which the main character begs the narrator of the epilogue to kill him, falls in love with the trader’s daughter Edvarda, and lives alone with his dog (*) Aesop. In another of this author’s novels, a harelipped woman strangles her harelipped infant daughter. This author of Mysteries wrote about Lieutenant Glahn. This author of Pan described the simple farm life of Inger and Isak in another novel. For 10 points, name this Hitler-loving Norwegian who won the Nobel for writing The Growth of the Soil.
tossups.question_sanitized: In one novel by this author, the protagonist forgoes the vial of prussic acid that he carries in his vest pocket by leaping off a jetty. The main character befriends the cripple Miniman and chases the lieutenant's bride Dagny in that novel by this author, who also created the entrepreneur Geissler, who staves off a drought by building an irrigation system. Besides writing a book about Johan Nagel, this author penned a novel in which the main character begs the narrator of the epilogue to kill him, falls in love with the trader's daughter Edvarda, and lives alone with his dog (*) Aesop. In another of this author's novels, a harelipped woman strangles her harelipped infant daughter. This author of Mysteries wrote about Lieutenant Glahn. This author of Pan described the simple farm life of Inger and Isak in another novel. For 10 points, name this Hitler-loving Norwegian who won the Nobel for writing The Growth of the Soil.

bonuses.leadin: In a novel by this author, Porfiry freezes to death on his mother’s grave after neglecting his entire family. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In a novel by this author, Porfiry freezes to death on his mother's grave after neglecting his entire family. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Russian author of the depressing novel The Golovlyov Family and the novel The History of a Town, which is set in Glupov, often translated as Foolsburg.

In his introduction to The Golovlyov Family, James Wood wrote that the “alienated actor” prototype runs from Dostoevsky, to Shchedrin, to this author. Wood cites this author’s novel Mysteries and a novel whose protagonist pawns his clothes and meets the woman Ylajali (“EE-lah-YAH-lee”).

When Shchedrin was upset at negative reviews, this author sent him a letter telling him he was “destined to make a deep mark on our literature.” This author’s own novels include First Love and Fathers and Sons.
parts_sanitized: Name this Russian author of the depressing novel The Golovlyov Family and the novel The History of a Town, which is set in Glupov, often translated as Foolsburg.

In his introduction to The Golovlyov Family, James Wood wrote that the "alienated actor" prototype runs from Dostoevsky, to Shchedrin, to this author. Wood cites this author's novel Mysteries and a novel whose protagonist pawns his clothes and meets the woman Ylajali ("EE-lah-YAH-lee").

When Shchedrin was upset at negative reviews, this author sent him a letter telling him he was "destined to make a deep mark on our literature." This author's own novels include First Love and Fathers and Sons.
answers: Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin [accept either underlined portion; or Nikolai Shchedrin; or Mikhail Yevgrafovich Saltykov-Shchedrin; or Mikhail Yevgrafovich Saltykov]
Knut Hamsun [or Knud Pedersen] (The other novel is Hunger. The Wood introduction is for the NYRB Classics edition of The Golovlyov Family.)
answers_sanitized: Mikhail Saltykov-Shchedrin [accept either underlined portion; or Nikolai Shchedrin; or Mikhail Yevgrafovich Saltykov-Shchedrin; or Mikhail Yevgrafovich Saltykov]
Knut Hamsun [or Knud Pedersen] (The other novel is Hunger. The Wood introduction is for the NYRB Classics edition of The Golovlyov Family.)

bonuses.leadin: In an interview with his ex-wife, an author from this country admitted that he had estranged many of his friends and relatives by depicting their private lives in his books. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country home to the author of the autofictional series My Struggle. Another author from this country wrote “new realist” novels like The Growth of the Soil and Hunger.
This author characterized Norwegian literature after the sagas as “four hundred years of darkness.” This Norwegian wrote plays like Ghosts and A Doll’s House.

Norwegian author Jon Fosse is best known for two novels about the painter Lars Hertevig titled for this feeling. A László Krasznahorkai novel titled for this feeling features a circus with the world’s largest dead whale.
answers: Kingdom of Norway (The two authors are Karl Ove Knausgård and Knut Hamsun.)
melancholy [or melancholia; accept Melancholia I; accept Melancholia II; accept The Melancholy of Resistance]
answers_sanitized: Kingdom of Norway (The two authors are Karl Ove Knausgard and Knut Hamsun.)
melancholy [or melancholia; accept Melancholia I; accept Melancholia II; accept The Melancholy of Resistance]

bonuses.leadin: After treason charges against this author were dropped due to his supposed mental impairment, this author wrote the book On Overgrown Paths to prove that his faculties were intact. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author whose most famous novel depicts a starving vagrant wandering the streets of Kristiania.

One of the writings that led to Hamsun’s treason trial was this very short work, published in Aftenposten(“OFF-tin-poh-stin”), which describes its subject as “a warrior for humankind and a preacher of the gospel of justice for all nations.” A description is acceptable.

Another Norwegian author, Karl Ove Knausgaard (“call OO-vuh kuh-NOWSS-gor”), included an over 400-page essay about Hitler in this six-novel cycle, which shares its title with an autobiographical work Hitler wrote while imprisoned.
answers: Hamsun’s obituary for Hitler [accept death notice for “obituary”; prompt on partial answer]
answers_sanitized: Knut Hamsun [or Knud Pedersen] (That novel is Hunger.)
Hamsun's obituary for Hitler [accept death notice for "obituary"; prompt on partial answer]
My Struggle [accept Min kamp; accept Mein Kampf]

parts_sanitized: Name this author whose most famous novel depicts a starving vagrant wandering the streets of Kristiania.

Another Norwegian author, Karl Ove Knausgaard ("call OO-vuh kuh-NOWSS-gor"), included an over 400-page essay about Hitler in this six-novel cycle, which shares its title with an autobiographical work Hitler wrote while imprisoned.

bonuses.leadin: Inger kills her newborn child, who has inherited her cleft lip, in a novel by this author. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this author who echoed Nazi ideals of ‘blood and soil’ in their novel Growth of the Soil and wrote another novel in which the protagonist wanders the streets of Kristiania at night.
Hamsun believed that ‘the Germans are fighting for us’ during World War II and wrote an obituary for this Nazi leader, describing him as ‘a preacher of the gospel of justice for all nations’.

Despite Hamsun’s politics, this other author felt that he was ‘the father of modern literature’. This author translated many of Hamsun’s works and wrote short stories like ‘The Spinoza of Market Street’.
answers_sanitized: Isaac Bashevis Singer [accept either underlined part]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Inger kills her newborn child, who has inherited her cleft lip, in a novel by this author. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this author who echoed Nazi ideals of 'blood and soil' in their novel Growth of the Soil and wrote another novel in which the protagonist wanders the streets of Kristiania at night.
Hamsun believed that 'the Germans are fighting for us' during World War II and wrote an obituary for this Nazi leader, describing him as 'a preacher of the gospel of justice for all nations'.

Despite Hamsun's politics, this other author felt that he was 'the father of modern literature'. This author translated many of Hamsun's works and wrote short stories like 'The Spinoza of Market Street'.

bonuses.leadin: A poem from this country warned against the rise of Nazism with the line “You must not tolerate so very well the injustice that does not affect yourself!” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country where “Dare not to sleep” was written. Another poem from this country tells of a sailor who rescues an English nobleman who had earlier captured him for smuggling food to try to save his family.
That poem, titled “Terje Vigen” (“TAIR-yuh VEE-gun”), was written by this author better known for plays like Ghosts and An Enemy of the People.

Unlike Arnulf Øverland (“OH-vur-lahn”), the leftist author of “Dare not to sleep,” this Norwegian author collaborated with Nazi Germany and notoriously wrote an obituary for Hitler calling him a “warrior for mankind.” He wrote a novel chronicling Isak and Inger’s development of their farm.
answers_sanitized: Kingdom of Norway [or Kongeriket Norge; or Kongeriket Noreg]

bonuses.leadin: The protagonist of this novel imagines his discovery of the word “Kuboa” and writes “1848” on every corner of his notebook in the hope that literary inspiration will come to him. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel whose narrator repeatedly brings his clothing to pawnbrokers, throws up food that he manages to buy, and encounters a woman whom he calls “Ylajali” (“ee-la-YAH-lee”).

Hunger was the breakthrough novel for this Norwegian winner of the 1920 Literature Nobel and author of Mysteries, in which the strange yellow-suit-wearing Johan Nagel intrigues a small town before his suicide.

In Mysteries, Nagel’s case for one of these instruments is revealed to contain dirty laundry. In Kafka’s “The Metamorphosis,” Gregor Samsa dreams of sending his sister Grete to music school to study this string instrument.

bonuses.leadin: Name these Norwegian novelists, for 10 points each.
parts: This author defended his support of Hitler as a “preacher of the gospel of all nations” in his last novel On Overgrown Paths and described Eleseus leaving the family farm in Sellanraa in The Growth of the Soil.
This author hailed as one of the “Four Greats” of Norwegian literature wrote The Fisher Maiden and provided the lyrics for Norway’s national anthem.

This best-selling author wrote about Jon and Trond grappling with the legacy of the Nazi occupation in his novel Out Stealing Horses, and James Wood reviewed his recent novel I Curse the River of Time.
